nativeRange.habitat: Large, slow to moderately flowing rivers and their tributaries; sandy or mixed sand-gravel substrates over white-sand banks and flooded forest margins; clear to blackwater conditions depending on drainage

decorPreferences: deep fine sand substrate (mandatory — minimum 8 cm depth for sifting behaviour)
large smooth river stones and driftwood as territory markers
robust or potted background plants (Amazon swords, Vallisneria) — avoid delicate foreground plants
open sand foraging areas in front, sheltered refuge zones behind decor
dim or moderate lighting preferred; shade areas with floating plants

diet.notes: The Altifrons Eartheater is an obligate sand-sifting forager in the wild, taking mouthfuls of substrate and sieving invertebrates, small crustaceans, insect larvae, worms, and organic detritus through specialised gill rakers before expelling the sand from the operculum (the bony plate covering the gills). In aquaria, the species generally accepts high-quality sinking pellets, frozen or live bloodworms, brine shrimp, white worms, daphnia, and spirulina-enriched foods. Gravel substrate MUST NOT be used — sharp gravel particles damage the fine gill rakers during sifting and can cause chronic injury. Feeding 2–3 times daily with a varied diet is recommended, including some vegetable matter such as blanched spinach or spirulina wafers.

goodWith: other large peaceful Amazonian cichlids (Satanoperca, other Geophagus spp.)
large South American tetras (Congo tetras too small — use Giant Danios, Bleeding Heart tetras, Silver Dollars)
large peaceful catfish (Ancistrus, Hypancistrus, Cory cats of comparable origin)
peaceful open-water South American cichlids of similar size
avoidWith: small fish and nano species (predation or harassment risk)
aggressive cichlids (Oscars, Midas, Red Devils) — will be bullied
bottom-dwelling territorial fish competing for same substrate zone
shrimp and small invertebrates (eaten during sand-sifting)
delicate substrate-rooting plants (uprooted by sifting activity)

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by DIFish; 2048x1365. Striking single adult under actinic/reef lighting; tack-sharp, whole fish framed, showing elongated red-tipped dorsal, iridescent blue-green lateral spangling, red-and-white spotted caudal, and filamentous pelvic extensions.

Photo by Ong Jyh Seng; 2048x1536. Group of several eartheaters under blue aquarium light, good for a shoaling species; slightly soft and carries a phone date/location text overlay, so kept as a non-recommended group shot.

Photo by Bill Merkley; 640x480. Daylight planted-tank pair over substrate showing blue-green spangling and high-fronted profile; lower resolution with a small date stamp, non-recommended supporting shot.

summary: The Altifrons Eartheater is a large Amazonian cichlid that earns its name by continuously sifting mouthfuls of fine sand to filter out invertebrates. Adults reach 18–22 cm across several geographic color forms, including Tapajós, Xingu, Tocantins, and Rio Negro variants. A 400 L or larger tank with a deep sand bed is essential; gravel will damage its gill rakers.
careNotes:
The Altifrons Eartheater (Geophagus altifrons) is a large, moderately demanding South American cichlid available in several geographically distinct colour forms — Tapajós, Xingu, Tocantins, and Rio Negro variants are frequently marketed separately in the specialist trade and are generally advised not to be mixed, as interbreeding between forms can compromise colour form purity and produce hybrid offspring. The species is commonly confused with Geophagus surinamensis, which is rarely imported; most fish sold as 'surinamensis' in the hobby are misidentified G. altifrons or related congeners.

The single most critical husbandry requirement is substrate: fine sand is mandatory. G. altifrons feeds by taking mouthfuls of sand, filtering invertebrates and organic matter through specialised gill rakers (comb-like structures inside the throat that strain food from the substrate), and expelling the sand from the operculum. Gravel or coarse substrate will physically lacerate these delicate gill rakers, leading to chronic injury, secondary infection, and death. A minimum sand bed depth of 8 cm is recommended to allow uninhibited sifting.

A 400 L tank with a minimum footprint of 150 cm is the practical floor for a small group of 4–6 individuals. Soft, warm, acidic to neutral water (pH 5.5–7.5, gH 1–12, 24–30°C) mirrors Amazonian river conditions and is the recommended target range. Regular large water changes (30–40% weekly) are essential, as sifting activity disturbs accumulated mulm and the bioload of this large cichlid is substantial. Filtration should be powerful but the output should be diffused — these fish tend to prefer slow-moving water.

G. altifrons is semi-aggressive within the cichlid community but is generally peaceful toward dissimilar tankmates that do not compete for substrate territory. Pair bonds can form within a group, and spawning pairs may become temporarily more territorial.
breedingNotes:
Geophagus altifrons is a larvophilous mouthbrooder: eggs are laid and fertilised on a flat surface (rock, root, or cleared patch of sand), and the female (or both parents) picks up the wriggling larvae — not the eggs — into the buccal cavity shortly after hatching. The larvae are incubated and protected in the mouth for several weeks until they are free-swimming fry capable of some independent foraging. This is distinct from immediate egg-mouthbrooding (ovophilous) behaviour seen in many African cichlids.

Conditioning the pair with varied live and frozen foods is recommended before spawning. A brood typically consists of 100–400 larvae. Fry can be raised in the main tank if sufficient hiding space exists, or moved to a rearing tank. First foods include baby brine shrimp, micro-worms, and finely powdered fry food. Parental care is extensive; fry are regularly recalled into the parents' mouths when threatened. Breeding is generally considered moderately challenging, given the tank size requirements, water quality demands, and careful dietary conditioning needed to bring a pair into spawning condition.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Geophagus altifrons
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range, pH, hardness, native range (Amazon basin — Tapajós, Xingu, Tocantins and Rio Negro drainages), biotope, substrate requirements, diet (sand-sifting, invertebrates), breeding mode (larvophilous mouthbrooder), and compatibility notes. Confirmed common misidentification with G. surinamensis.

additional.name: FishBase — Geophagus altifrons
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 22 cm TL), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Brazil, Amazon basin), temperature range (22–30°C), and diet classification (omnivore/invertivore). Confirmed species is distinct from G. surinamensis.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Eartheater Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-hobby perspective. Confirmed sand-only substrate requirement, tank size guidance (75–125 gal for large eartheaters), temperature preference (78–86°F / 25.5–30°C), diet (sinking pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp), semi-aggressive temperament, and group-keeping recommendation. Notes the gill-raker gravel-damage risk explicitly.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Geophagus Guide
additional.notes: UK hobbyist editorial source. Covered geographic colour forms sold under the altifrons/surinamensis trade names, larvophilous mouthbrooding behaviour, water parameter targets (pH 6.0–7.2, soft water), and compatibility with large South American tetras and catfish. Confirmed multiple geographic forms are sold separately in the specialist trade.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Geophagus altifrons Species Profile
additional.notes: Specialist cichlid community resource. Supplementary detail on breeding behaviour (larvophilous vs ovophilous distinction), brood size (100–400 fry), parental behaviour, fry first foods, and conspecific geographic form notes. Confirmed minimum group size of 4 for stable social dynamics.

fish.breedingNotes: The Altifrons Eartheater is a larvophilous mouthbrooder, meaning eggs are laid and fertilised on a flat surface and it is the wriggling larvae — not the eggs — that are taken into the buccal cavity (mouth pouch) shortly after hatching. Brood sizes typically range from 100 to 400 larvae. Parental care tends to be extensive, with fry regularly recalled into the parents' mouths when threatened. Conditioning the pair with a varied diet of live and frozen foods is recommended before attempting to spawn the species.
